observations: High fever with severe joint and muscle pain
Possible platelet drop — monitor closely
Rash may appear after 2–3 days of fever
advice: Visit a doctor immediately for a blood test (CBC / NS1 Antigen)
Drink plenty of fluids and ORS solution to stay hydrated
Take Paracetamol only — avoid Aspirin and Ibuprofen
Monitor platelet count every 24 hours
Rest completely and avoid mosquito bites to stop spread

observations: Fever with generalized body pain and fatigue
Likely viral origin — no antibiotics needed
Symptoms usually resolve in 5–7 days
advice: Take Paracetamol to control fever (every 6 hours if needed)
Drink warm fluids — water, soups, herbal tea
Take complete bed rest for 2–3 days
Avoid cold foods and direct AC air
Visit a doctor if fever exceeds 103°F or lasts more than 5 days

observations: Upper respiratory tract infection detected
Sneezing and nasal discharge indicate viral cold
Usually self-limiting within 7 days
advice: Inhale steam twice a day to clear nasal congestion
Drink warm water with honey and ginger
Take antihistamines if you have runny nose and sneezing
Avoid cold drinks and ice cream
Gargle with warm salt water for sore throat relief

observations: Headache without fever indicates tension or migraine
Light sensitivity and nausea suggest migraine episode
Stress and dehydration are common triggers
advice: Rest in a dark, quiet room away from screens
Apply cold or warm compress on forehead/neck
Stay well-hydrated — drink at least 2–3 litres of water
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and strong perfumes
Take pain relief medication if pain is severe
Track your triggers in a headache diary

observations: Rash without fever suggests allergic or contact dermatitis
Could be triggered by food, soap, or environmental allergens
Monitor if rash spreads or is accompanied by breathing difficulty
advice: Apply calamine lotion or hydrocortisone cream on the rash
Take an antihistamine (cetirizine) to reduce itching
Avoid scratching — it can cause infection
Identify and avoid the allergen (food, soap, cloth, etc.)
Go to emergency if you feel throat tightness or breathing difficulty (anaphylaxis)

observations: Joint pain without fever suggests arthritis or muscle strain
Morning stiffness is a hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis
Sudden severe joint swelling may indicate gout
advice: Apply warm compress to affected joints for 15 minutes
Gentle stretching and physiotherapy exercises daily
Avoid sitting in one position for long hours
Maintain healthy weight to reduce joint pressure
Consult doctor for X-ray or blood tests (RA Factor, Uric Acid)
Avoid self-medicating with strong painkillers long-term

observations: Nausea, vomiting and diarrhea indicate GI infection
Likely caused by contaminated food or water
Risk of dehydration — monitor fluid intake carefully
advice: Drink ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) every 20 minutes
Eat bland food — rice, banana, toast, boiled vegetables
Avoid dairy, spicy food, and caffeine until fully recovered
Take probiotics to restore gut bacteria
Go to hospital if vomiting lasts more than 12 hours or blood in stool

observations: Cyclic fever with chills and sweating is a classic malaria pattern
Transmitted by Anopheles mosquito bites
Blood smear test (Peripheral Smear) is needed for confirmation
advice: Visit a doctor immediately for a malaria rapid test or blood smear
Take antimalarial drugs only as prescribed by a doctor
Drink plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration
Use mosquito nets and repellents to prevent spread
Complete the full course of antimalarial medication even if feeling better

observations: Sustained high fever with stomach pain suggests typhoid
Caused by Salmonella Typhi bacteria via contaminated food/water
Widal test or blood culture needed for diagnosis
advice: See a doctor immediately — typhoid needs antibiotic treatment
Eat only soft, easy-to-digest food (khichdi, curd rice, soups)
Drink boiled and cooled water only
Avoid raw vegetables, street food, and outside water
Complete the full antibiotic course — do not stop early

observations: Body pain without fever suggests overexertion or muscle strain
Fatigue may also be due to poor sleep or nutritional deficiency
Monitor if pain is localized to one area — could be injury
advice: Take complete rest for 1–2 days
Apply warm compress or heating pad to sore muscles
Stay hydrated and eat protein-rich foods
Do light stretching after rest
Take Vitamin B12 and Iron supplements if fatigue is chronic
Consult a doctor if weakness persists more than a week

observations: Fever with cough and loss of taste/smell are COVID-19 warning signs
Breathing difficulty warrants immediate medical attention
Self-isolate immediately to prevent community spread
advice: Get a COVID-19 antigen test or RT-PCR done immediately
Self-isolate in a well-ventilated room for at least 7–10 days
Monitor SpO2 (oxygen level) using a pulse oximeter — below 94% = emergency
Take Paracetamol for fever and body pain
Stay well-hydrated and eat nutritious food
Call emergency services if breathing becomes labored
